question: True or False? If ($x_n$) is bounded and diverges, then there exist two subsequences of ($x_n$) that converge to different limits. Answer True or False.

conversation.content: True or False? If ($x_n$) is bounded and diverges, then there exist two subsequences of ($x_n$) that converge to different limits. Answer True or False.

True.

Proof: By Bolzano–Weierstrass a bounded sequence has a convergent subsequence with limit L. If every convergent subsequence had limit L, then the whole sequence would converge to L (contradiction). Hence there exist two subsequences with distinct limits.

then the answer: True

True.

Proof: By Bolzano–Weierstrass a bounded sequence has a convergent subsequence with limit L. If every convergent subsequence had limit L then every subsequence would have a further subsequence converging to L, which implies the whole sequence converges to L — contradiction. Hence there is another convergent subsequence with limit ≠ L.

then the answer: True

True.

Since (x_n) is bounded, Bolzano–Weierstrass gives a convergent subsequence x_{n_k} → L. If every convergent subsequence had limit L, then for any ε>0 only finitely many terms could satisfy |x_n−L|≥ε, so x_n→L, contradicting divergence. Hence there must be another subsequence with limit ≠L.

then the answer: True

True.

Since (x_n) is bounded, Bolzano–Weierstrass gives a convergent subsequence x_{n_k} → L. If every subsequence that converges had limit L then x_n would converge to L: contrarily, if x_n did not converge to L there is ε>0 and infinitely many n with |x_n−L|≥ε, hence a subsequence with |x_{m_k}−L|≥ε; by Bolzano–Weierstrass that subsequence has a convergent sub-subsequence with limit L' satisfying |L'−L|≥ε>0, contradiction. Thus there exist two subsequences converging to different limits.

then the answer: True

True.

Proof: By Bolzano–Weierstrass, since (x_n) is bounded there is a subsequence x_{n_k} → L. If every subsequence that converges had limit L, then for every ε>0 only finitely many terms could satisfy |x_n−L|≥ε (otherwise those infinitely many terms yield, by Bolzano–Weierstrass, a subsequence converging to some L' with |L'−L|≥ε), which exactly means x_n→L, contradicting divergence. Hence there exists a subsequence converging to some L'≠L, so two subsequences converge to different limits.

then the answer: True

True.

Since (x_n) is bounded, Bolzano–Weierstrass gives a convergent subsequence x_{n_k} → L. If every convergent subsequence had limit L, then for any ε>0 the set {n: |x_n − L| ≥ ε} must be finite (otherwise it contains infinitely many indices and hence, by Bolzano–Weierstrass, a subsequence with a limit L' satisfying |L'−L|≥ε), which exactly means x_n → L, contradicting divergence. Therefore there exists a subsequence converging to some L' ≠ L, so two subsequences converge to different limits.

then the answer: True
